title: Chapter 4: The Sterling Response
content: The woman in the mirror is perfect.

Vivienne Sterling-Hargrove stands motionless before the full-length glass in the dressing room of the Bel Air mansion and studies the stranger wearing her face. Someone else has dressed her — a stylist, probably, a thin woman with efficient hands who spoke in soft affirmations while pinning and tugging and smoothing. Vivienne can't remember the woman's name. There had been coffee offered, and a brief discussion about neckline architecture, and then the woman was gone and Vivienne was alone with herself.

The dress is Schiaparelli. Fourteen thousand dollars of silk faille and structural boning, the color of barely-there champagne, the kind of neutral that costs more than any color. It fits perfectly. Every seam lies flat against her torso, every dart cradles a curve with mathematical precision. The fabric presses against her ribs like fingers — not gentle, not cruel, just constant. She can feel each stitch. The boning at her waist. The silk lining sliding against her skin with every shallow breath she's been trained to take.
A cage, then. The most beautiful cage she's ever worn.

She turns slightly. The mirror catches the line of her jaw, the pale sweep of her collarbone, the way the dress makes her posture impossible to slouch. Her hair is pinned back — not by her own hand, never by her own hand anymore — in a chignon that cost more than most people's rent. Her makeup is seamless. Her expression is serene.
She hates every atom of it.

The Le Labo Santal 33 on her wrists rises in the warmth of the room — sandalwood, cedar, leather, violet. Androgynous and expensive, the kind of scent that makes people lean in, confused. Tom had called it "too masculine" when she first started wearing it, his nose wrinkling like she'd brought home the wrong grocery item. She'd kept wearing it. The only rebellion she could afford.

Vivienne presses her palm flat against her stomach. The silk holds her in. The silk holds her together. Underneath, her heart beats too fast, has beat too fast for years, a hummingbird trapped in the same cage as everything else.
She is still standing there when the front door opens at 11 PM.

Tom doesn't announce himself. He never does. The sound travels through the mansion's open floor plan — the click of the lock, the shuffle of Italian leather on limestone, the particular way he drops his keys on the console table with the performative exhaustion of a man who wants to be asked about his day.
Vivienne doesn't ask.

She's in the living room, still in the Schiaparelli, seated on the edge of the sectional with her phone in her lap. The dress makes sitting an exercise in controlled breathing. She hasn't changed. She hasn't moved from this spot in over an hour. The mansion is so quiet she can hear the refrigerator cycling in the kitchen forty feet away.

Tom rounds the corner. He's still in his suit — charcoal, fitted, the one from the Wilshire meeting — with his tie loosened and his top button undone. He doesn't look at her. He walks past the sectional toward the bar cart, fingers working at his collar, and the cloud of him drifts in his wake.

Vanilla. Synthetic rose. The combination is instant and chemical, the kind of fragrance that clings to fabric from close contact. It's not Vivienne's scent. It's not any scent that belongs in this house. It's cheap and sweet and young, the kind of thing you'd find at a mall kiosk, the kind of thing a woman would spray on before a married man's hotel room.
Vivienne's spine goes rigid. The boning in the dress presses harder against her ribs.
Tom pours himself two fingers of Japanese whisky. He takes a sip. He still hasn't looked at her.

This is the arrangement. She knows what it is. She's known since the second year, when the first perfume wasn't Le Labo, when the first late night became a pattern, when she stopped asking and he stopped pretending to be sorry. The arrangement is simple: she is Mrs. Thomas Hargrove IV, and she performs that role with precision, and in exchange she gets this — the mansion, the name, the Schiaparelli dresses fitted by women whose names she can't remember, and the privilege of not being asked to feel anything about any of it.
She swipes open her phone. She needs something to look at that isn't him.
Jax's party is everywhere.

The algorithm, that omniscient gossip, has decided Vivienne needs to see it. Every feed, every story, every carousel — the same cascade of impossible images. Holographic whales breaching through the Malibu skyline, their bodies made of fractured light, swimming through clouds that don't exist. Influencers posing with their mouths open, silhouetted against digital auroras. A drone shot of the Pacific, dark and enormous, lit from above by things that aren't stars.
And Jax. Jax Vance. The crypto queen in her element.
Vivienne's thumb slows.

In one photo, Jax is laughing — head thrown back, hair catching light that seems to exist just for her, one hand raised mid-gesture as if she's conducting the universe. The rings on her fingers catch the holographic glow. She looks elemental. She looks like someone who has never, not once, worn a dress that felt like a cage.

Vivienne keeps scrolling. A video clip — the whales again, their song mixing with a bassline that throbs even through phone speakers. A carousel of outfits, each more elaborate than the last. A shot of the yacht lights on the dark water.
Then she stops.

It's a candid. Someone caught Jax in profile, looking away from the party, toward the ocean. The holographic light paints half her face blue and violet. The other half is shadow. Her expression is unreadable — not the performative joy of the other photos, not the magnetic energy she wears like skin. Something else. Something that looks almost like searching.
Like she's looking toward the water. Looking for something on the other side of it.
Vivienne's thumb hovers. The image burns on the screen. She can feel her own pulse in the pad of her finger, pressing against the glass.
She knows what's on the other side of the bay. She knows it the way you know a wound — not because you think about it, but because it aches when the weather changes.
"Obsessed with that crypto trash?"
Tom's voice is light. Teasing. The voice he uses at dinner parties when he's dismissing something beneath him.

He crosses the room in three strides — Vivienne counts them like a meditation, one-two-three — and his hand closes around the phone before she can react. His grip is white-knuckle. His thumb presses into the screen hard enough that Vivienne can see the pressure distort the image of Jax's face.
His smile doesn't reach his eyes. It never does.
"You're always on that thing," he says, still light, still teasing, his tone the verbal equivalent of a shrug. "You know what that does to your sleep, Viv."
He throws the phone.

Not across the room — across the room would be careless, would be something she could describe to someone later. He throws it at the marble wall beside the fireplace with the specific, practiced angle of a man who knows exactly where it will land and how it will sound. The crack is sharp, percussive, like a bone breaking. The screen spiderwebs — a constellation of fractures blooming from the point of impact. The phone clatters to the limestone floor and lies there, face up, Jax's profile still glowing through the broken glass.

Tom crosses to her. He cups the back of her head with one hand — his fingers in her pinned hair, his palm warm and sure — and kisses her forehead. Hard. His lips press against her skin with a firmness that isn't affection. It's a seal. A stamp. The way you close an envelope.
"Get some sleep, Viv." His breath is warm and whisky-sour against her hairline. "Big day tomorrow."
Then he walks to the bedroom. The door closes behind him with the soft, expensive click of solid walnut.
Vivienne doesn't follow.
She sits. The dress holds her. The mansion holds her. The sound of the phone hitting the wall plays on a loop in the dark theater of her skull.

After a long time — she doesn't know how long; the only clock in the living room is decorative, a sculpture of time that doesn't actually tell it — Vivienne stands. The Schiaparelli rustles against the leather of the sectional as she moves, a sound like a whispered complaint. She walks to the phone. She bends down. The marble floor is cold on her bare feet, the kind of cold that seeps through the skin and into the cavity of the chest.
She picks up the phone.

The screen is shattered but it's still alive, the LCD bleeding light through a hundred tiny canyons. Jax's Instagram post glows through the wreckage — the photo of the holographic jellyfish, its tendrils drifting through impossible water, colors that don't have names shifting along its edges. Below it, the caption: "Everything beautiful is a signal. You just have to know the frequency."

Vivienne's index finger traces the cracked glass. The broken surface catches her fingerprint, tiny ridges against tiny ravines. She traces over the jellyfish, over the spectrum of light, over the shape of the words. Then her finger moves to the profile photo in the corner — Jax's face, smaller than her thumbnail, infinitely larger than the room.
She opens her messages.

The thread is empty. The last message was sent from this phone two years ago, a single line: *I can't do this anymore.* The message before that, from the other side, from a number that is no longer active: *Then don't.* And after that: nothing. Two years of nothing. The conversation is a closed wound, healed ugly, the scar tissue thick and immobile.

Vivienne's thumb hovers over the keyboard. She almost types something. She can feel the shape of the words in her tendons, in the nerves that connect intention to action. I saw you. I saw you looking at the water. I know what you're looking for. I'm looking for it too.
Almost.
She puts the phone face-down on the bar cart. The cracked screen clicks against the walnut tray.
She walks to the window.

The Bel Air mansion faces the hills, not the ocean. The architect — Tom's choice, naturally — oriented every room toward the quiet green slopes and the neighbor's manicured olive groves. Safe. Contained. No water in sight. The Pacific is on the other side of the ridge, miles of canyon and fog and geography between Vivienne and the open blue, and from here she can't see it, can't hear it, can't smell it.
But she knows it's there.

She presses her forehead against the glass. It's cold. The condensation from her breath blooms and fades, blooms and fades. The hills are dark except for the sparse amber of security lights. Somewhere below, in the canyon, a coyote is probably hunting. Somewhere on the other side of the ridge, the ocean is doing what the ocean does — pulling in, pushing out, relentless and indifferent and alive.

She holds her left hand up against the window. The wedding ring catches the moonlight — three carats of conflict-free brilliant-cut in a platinum setting, the stone so clear it looks like trapped water. It throws a tiny rainbow across the glass. She's always found that cruel. A prism on a prison.

She imagines what it would feel like to take it off. The specific resistance of the knuckle. The sudden lightness of the fourth finger. The pale band of skin underneath that has never seen the sun, a negative space in the shape of a promise.

She imagines setting it on the windowsill. She imagines walking away from it. She imagines the coyote in the canyon, the salt in the air, the lights of a house she's never been to on a beach she's never walked.
Her hand drops.
Not yet.

Vivienne crosses to the bedroom — not the master, not where Tom is sleeping the righteous sleep of a man who has never once doubted his right to occupy space — but her dressing room, the one with the mirror and the Schiaparelli and the ghost of a stylist whose name she can't remember.
She sits at the vanity. She opens the jewelry box.

The box is a masterwork of walnut and brass, Japanese-made, the kind of object that exists to be admired rather than used. Inside, arranged on cream suede, the pieces of a life performed: Cartier Love bracelet, heritage pearls from the Sterling side, a Van Cleef Alhambra necklace Tom gave her for their anniversary (the one where she'd cried in the bathroom for forty minutes and told him it was allergies), a smattering of gifts and acquisitions that together represent more money than her mother made in a decade of teaching high school English in Akron.
Under all of it, pressed flat against the suede bottom, hidden beneath the velvet lining she'd loosened with a seam ripper three years ago, there is a cheap rubber bracelet.

A festival wristband. The kind that fastens with a plastic snap and fades in the shower and leaves a tan line if you keep it on long enough. The ink is nearly gone — six years of oils and friction and careful preservation have worn it to a whisper, but she can still read it in the right light: AKRON ARTS FEST 2019.

She'd been twenty-two. She'd been wearing cutoffs and a tank top and sunscreen that smelled like coconut and she'd been happy — actually, genuinely, stupidly happy — standing in a crowd of strangers while a band she'd never heard of played something loud and bright. And a woman had bumped into her, laughing, apologizing, and their eyes had met, and the world had done something it had never done before.
It had opened.

Vivienne pulls the wristband from its hiding place. She fastens it around her right wrist, over the vein, under the cuff of the Schiaparelli where no one will see. The rubber is soft with age. The plastic snap clicks shut with a sound like a tiny, defiant heartbeat.

She looks at herself in the vanity mirror. Fourteen thousand dollars of silk caging her ribs. The heritage pearls at her throat. The Van Cleef around her neck. And hidden under all of it, against her pulse, Akron, Ohio, 2019, a version of herself that still exists like a star that's died but whose light is still traveling.
She reaches for the shattered phone.

The screen bleeds light through its fractures. She opens her messages. She finds the number — the one she's never deleted, the one that lives in her contacts under a single initial, the one that Tom doesn't know about because Tom has never thought to check because Tom has never once considered that something in this house might not belong to him.
Her thumb finds the keyboard. She types one word.
Seen.
She presses send before she can think. Before the cage can close. Before the silk and the marble and the three-carat prison can convince her otherwise.
The message sends. The delivery confirmation glows. Read receipts are off. She'll never know if it arrives. She'll never know if anyone is listening on the other side of the bay.
She puts the phone down.

And across the water — she can't see it, she'll never see it from this room, but she feels it, the way you feel a sound in your chest before your ears register it — a green laser pulses against the Malibu sky.
Once.
Waiting.
